questions.question:
Consider the following set of processes that need to be scheduled on a single CPU. All the times are given in milliseconds.

Process Name | Arrival Time | Execution Time
A            | 0            | 6
B            | 2            | 2
C            | 4            | 4
D            | 5            | 3
E            | 8            | 3

Using the Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) scheduling algorithm, the average process turnaround time (in msec) is ____.
questions.to_find: Average turnaround time of all processes under SRTF scheduling

understand.plain: We have 5 processes arriving at different times, each needing a certain amount of CPU time. SRTF is a preemptive version of Shortest Job First where the currently running process is preempted if a newly arrived process has a shorter remaining burst time. We need to simulate the CPU schedule and compute the average of (completion time - arrival time) for all processes.

keywords.explain: A preemptive scheduling algorithm where at every instant the process with the least remaining CPU burst time runs. A running process can be preempted by a newly arrived process with a shorter burst.
keywords.example: Process A has 4ms remaining; Process B arrives with burst 2ms. B preempts A.

keywords.explain: Total time a process spends in the system from arrival until completion. TAT = Completion Time - Arrival Time.
keywords.example: Process arrives at t=2, completes at t=6. TAT = 6-2 = 4ms.

keywords.explain: Forcibly removing a process from the CPU before it finishes its burst because a higher-priority (shorter remaining) process arrives.
keywords.example: A is running; B arrives with shorter remaining time; B takes over the CPU.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='110' viewBox='0 0 520 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='30' width='40' height='40' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>A</text><rect x='40' y='30' width='80' height='40' fill='#B4EED4' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='80' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B</text><rect x='120' y='30' width='160' height='40' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>A(cont)</text><rect x='280' y='30' width='120' height='40' fill='#F6D6C8' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='340' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>D</text><rect x='400' y='30' width='120' height='40' fill='#FFF0B2' stroke='#C8A000' stroke-width='2'/><text x='460' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>E</text><text x='0' y='90' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='38' y='90' class='sub mut'>2</text><text x='118' y='90' class='sub mut'>4</text><text x='278' y='90' class='sub mut'>8</text><text x='398' y='90' class='sub mut'>11</text><text x='515' y='90' class='sub mut'>14</text><text x='200' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Gantt Chart (SRTF)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Gantt chart showing SRTF execution: A runs 0-2, B runs 2-4, A resumes 4-8, D runs 8-11, E runs 11-14, then C runs 14-18

given.aim: Find the average turnaround time using SRTF scheduling for 5 processes

terms.meaning: The times at which each process enters the ready queue
terms.example: Process A arrives at t=0, B at t=2, etc.

terms.meaning: CPU time each process needs to complete
terms.connects: Shorter remaining burst causes preemption in SRTF

terms.meaning: The time when a process fully finishes execution
terms.example: B completes at t=4, so TAT_B = 4-2 = 2ms
terms.connects: Used to calculate turnaround time: TAT = CT - Arrival

terms.meaning: The mean of all individual turnaround times
terms.example: Avg TAT = (TAT_A + TAT_B + TAT_C + TAT_D + TAT_E) / 5

given.plan: Simulate SRTF step by step: at each arrival event, compare new burst with remaining bursts in the system. Preempt if new arrival is shorter. Record completion times. Compute TAT = CT - Arrival for each process, then average.

steps.title: Simulate SRTF execution
steps.formula_raw: At each arrival, preempt running process if new_burst < remaining_burst
steps.apply:
t=0-2: Only A in queue. A runs. A_rem = 6 - 2 = 4.
t=2: B arrives (burst=2). A_rem=4 > 2. B preempts A.
t=2-4: B runs. B_rem = 0. B completes at t=4. CT_B=4.
t=4: B done. C arrives (burst=4). A_rem=4. Tie: continue A (A has earlier arrival time).
t=4-5: A runs. A_rem = 3.
t=5: D arrives (burst=3). A_rem=3 = D=3. Tie: A arrived earlier. A continues.
t=5-8: A runs. A_rem = 0. A completes at t=8. CT_A=8.
t=8: E arrives (burst=3). Queue: C(4), D(3), E(3). D arrived first among 3ms jobs. D runs.
t=8-11: D runs. CT_D=11.
t=11: E runs (3 < 4).
t=11-14: E runs. CT_E=14.
t=14-18: C runs. CT_C=18.
steps.note: On ties in remaining time, the process with the earlier arrival time is preferred.

steps.title: Compute turnaround times
steps.apply:
TAT_A = 8 - 0 = 8 ms
TAT_B = 4 - 2 = 2 ms
TAT_C = 18 - 4 = 14 ms
TAT_D = 11 - 5 = 6 ms
TAT_E = 14 - 8 = 6 ms
Sum = 8 + 2 + 14 + 6 + 6 = 36 ms
Average = 36 / 5 = 7.2 ms
steps.note: C has the longest turnaround because it keeps getting preempted by shorter jobs.

questions.formula_note: SRTF (Shortest Remaining Time First) preemptive scheduling: at each arrival event compare new burst with remaining CPU time; turnaround time = completion time - arrival time.

questions.question:
Three processes P1, P2, and P3 each execute a loop of 100 iterations. In each iteration of the loop, a process performs a single computation that requires t_s CPU milliseconds and then initiates a single I/O operation that lasts for t_IO milliseconds. It is assumed that the computer where the processes execute has sufficient number of I/O devices and the OS of the computer assigns different I/O devices to each process. Also, the scheduling overhead of the OS is negligible. The processes have the following characteristics:

Process | t_s   | t_IO
P1 (A)  | 100   | 500
P2 (B)  | 350   | 500
P3 (C)  | 200   | 500

The processes P1, P2, and P3 are started at times 0, 5, and 10 milliseconds, in a pure time sharing system (round robin scheduling) that uses a time slice of 50 milliseconds. The time in milliseconds at which process P3 (C) would complete its first I/O operation is ____.
questions.to_find: Time at which process P3 (C) completes its first I/O operation

understand.plain: Three processes run with round robin scheduling using a 50ms time quantum. Each process needs a certain CPU time followed by an I/O operation. We must track when process C (needing 200ms CPU) finishes all its CPU work for the first iteration, starts its I/O, and then finishes that I/O. The I/O duration is 500ms for all processes.

keywords.explain: Each process gets a fixed time quantum (slice) in cyclic order. After using its quantum, the process is moved to the back of the ready queue.
keywords.example: With quantum=50ms, A runs 50ms, then B runs 50ms, then C runs 50ms, then A again.

keywords.explain: The maximum CPU time a process can use before being preempted and placed at the end of the ready queue.
keywords.example: Quantum = 50ms means no process runs for more than 50ms continuously.

keywords.explain: In each loop iteration, a process first does CPU work (t_s ms total) then does I/O (t_IO ms). The CPU work may require multiple time quanta.
keywords.example: C needs 200ms CPU = 4 quanta of 50ms each before it can start I/O.

UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='10' width='50' height='30' fill='#B4EED4' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='1'/><text x='25' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>A(50)</text><rect x='50' y='10' width='50' height='30' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><text x='75' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B(50)</text><rect x='100' y='10' width='50' height='30' fill='#F6D6C8' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='1'/><text x='125' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C(50)</text><rect x='150' y='10' width='50' height='30' fill='#B4EED4' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='1'/><text x='175' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>A(50)</text><rect x='200' y='10' width='50' height='30' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><text x='225' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B(50)</text><rect x='250' y='10' width='50' height='30' fill='#F6D6C8' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='1'/><text x='275' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C(50)</text><rect x='300' y='10' width='50' height='30' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><text x='325' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B(50)</text><rect x='350' y='10' width='50' height='30' fill='#F6D6C8' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='1'/><text x='375' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C(50)</text><rect x='400' y='10' width='50' height='30' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><text x='425' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B(50)</text><rect x='450' y='10' width='50' height='30' fill='#F6D6C8' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='475' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C(50)</text><text x='0' y='60' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='95' y='60' class='sub mut'>100</text><text x='195' y='60' class='sub mut'>200</text><text x='295' y='60' class='sub mut'>300</text><text x='395' y='60' class='sub mut'>400</text><text x='495' y='60' class='sub mut'>500</text><text x='475' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>C starts I/O at t=500</text><text x='200' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>I/O duration = 500ms -> C I/O complete at t=1000ms</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Round robin Gantt chart showing: A(0-50), B(50-100), C(100-150), A(150-200 finishes CPU), B(200-250), C(250-300), B(300-350), C(350-400), B(400-450), C(450-500 finishes CPU, starts I/O). C's I/O completes at t=500+500=1000ms.

given.aim: Find the time at which process C (P3) completes its first I/O operation

terms.meaning: Total CPU time each process needs for one loop iteration before starting I/O
terms.example: C needs t_s = 200ms of CPU time before its first I/O
terms.connects: Determines how many 50ms quanta C needs: 200/50 = 4 quanta

terms.meaning: Duration of the I/O operation in each loop iteration
terms.connects: C's I/O completes at (time C starts I/O) + 500

terms.meaning: A starts at t=0, B starts at t=5, C starts at t=10 ms
terms.example: C enters the ready queue at t=10, not t=0
terms.connects: Affects the order in which processes first get CPU time

terms.meaning: Each process gets at most 50ms of CPU per turn in round robin
terms.connects: C needs 4 full quanta (4 x 50 = 200ms) to complete its CPU work

given.plan: Simulate round robin scheduling with quantum=50ms. Track when each process uses the CPU. When C accumulates 200ms of CPU time, it starts I/O. Add 500ms to get C's I/O completion time.

steps.title: Simulate round robin scheduling
steps.formula_raw: Process gets CPU for min(quantum, remaining_burst) ms per turn
steps.apply:
A enters at t=0, B at t=5, C at t=10. Quantum = 50ms.

t=0-50: A runs. A_CPU_done=50. (B and C arrive during this slot)
t=50-100: B runs. B_CPU_done=50.
t=100-150: C runs. C_CPU_done=50.
t=150-200: A runs. A_CPU_done=100. A needs 100ms total -> A finishes CPU! A goes to I/O at t=200.
t=200-250: B runs. B_CPU_done=100.
t=250-300: C runs. C_CPU_done=100.
t=300-350: B runs. B_CPU_done=150.
t=350-400: C runs. C_CPU_done=150.
t=400-450: B runs. B_CPU_done=200. B needs 350ms, not done yet. (Actually B continues)
t=450-500: C runs. C_CPU_done=200. C needs 200ms -> C finishes CPU! C goes to I/O at t=500.
steps.note: A re-enters the ready queue from I/O at t=200+500=700ms, but this does not affect C's CPU completion since C finishes at t=500 before A returns.

steps.title: Compute C's first I/O completion time
steps.apply:
C starts I/O at t=500.
C's I/O duration = 500ms.
C's first I/O completes at t = 500 + 500 = 1000ms.
steps.note: Each process uses a separate I/O device so there is no I/O contention.

solution.result: Process C completes its first I/O operation at t = 1000 ms

questions.formula_note: Round Robin scheduling with time quantum: each process receives CPU for at most one quantum per turn; total CPU time needed divided by quantum gives number of turns required before I/O.

questions.question:
An operating system uses Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) scheduling algorithm for pre-emptive scheduling of processes. Consider the following set of processes with their arrival times and CPU burst times (in milliseconds):

Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time
P1      | 0            | 12
P2      | 2            | 4
P3      | 3            | 6
P4      | 8            | 5

The average waiting time (in milliseconds) of the processes is ____.
questions.to_find: Average waiting time of all processes under SRTF scheduling

understand.plain: Four processes arrive at different times and need varying amounts of CPU time. SRTF is preemptive — when a new process arrives with a shorter burst than the remaining time of the running process, the running process is preempted. We simulate the schedule, find when each process completes, then compute waiting time = turnaround time - burst time for each, and average them.

keywords.explain: Total time a process spends waiting in the ready queue (not running, not in I/O). Waiting Time = Turnaround Time - Burst Time = (Completion Time - Arrival Time) - Burst Time.
keywords.example: Process arrives at t=2, bursts for 4ms, completes at t=6. WT = (6-2)-4 = 0ms.

keywords.explain: When a new process arrives, its burst time is compared to the remaining CPU time of the running process. If new burst < remaining, the running process is preempted.
keywords.example: P1 has 10ms remaining; P2 arrives with burst 4ms. 4 < 10, so P2 preempts P1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='540' height='110' viewBox='0 0 540 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='30' width='40' height='40' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P1</text><rect x='40' y='30' width='80' height='40' fill='#B4EED4' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='80' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P2</text><rect x='120' y='30' width='120' height='40' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='180' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P1</text><rect x='240' y='30' width='120' height='40' fill='#FFF0B2' stroke='#C8A000' stroke-width='2'/><text x='300' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P3</text><rect x='360' y='30' width='100' height='40' fill='#F6D6C8' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='410' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P4</text><rect x='460' y='30' width='80' height='40' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='500' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P1</text><text x='0' y='90' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='38' y='90' class='sub mut'>2</text><text x='118' y='90' class='sub mut'>6</text><text x='238' y='90' class='sub mut'>12</text><text x='358' y='90' class='sub mut'>17</text><text x='458' y='90' class='sub mut'>22? No</text><text x='200' y='15' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>SRTF Gantt Chart</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Gantt chart: P1 runs 0-2, P2 preempts and runs 2-6 (completes), P1 resumes 6-12 (P3 added at 3 but P1 still shorter), P3 runs 12-17 (after P4 arrives at 8 but P3 rem < P4), P4 runs 17-22, P1 resumes 22-27

given.aim: Find the average waiting time for 4 processes under SRTF preemptive scheduling

terms.meaning: P1 arrives first and needs the most CPU time
terms.example: P1 will likely be preempted multiple times by shorter jobs
terms.connects: P1 accumulates the most waiting time

terms.meaning: P2 arrives at t=2 with only 4ms burst
terms.example: At t=2, P1 has 10ms remaining; 4 < 10 so P2 preempts P1
terms.connects: P2 runs to completion immediately after arrival — zero waiting time

terms.example: P2 has 3ms remaining when P3 arrives; P2 is shorter, so P2 keeps running
terms.connects: P3 waits until P2 completes (t=6), then competes with P1

terms.example: At t=8, P1 has rem=6, P3 has rem=4; P4=5 > P3_rem=4, so P3 continues
terms.connects: P4 waits until P3 completes at t=12, then runs

given.plan: Simulate SRTF: track remaining burst for each process. At each arrival, if new burst < current remaining, preempt. Record completion times. Compute WT = CT - Arrival - Burst for each, then average.

steps.title: Simulate SRTF execution
steps.formula_raw: Preempt if new_burst < remaining_burst of current process
steps.apply:
t=0: Only P1. P1 runs. P1_rem=12.
t=2: P2 arrives (burst=4). P1_rem=10. 4 < 10 -> P2 preempts P1. P2 runs.
t=3: P3 arrives (burst=6). P2_rem=3. 3 < 6 -> P2 keeps running.
t=6: P2 done. CT_P2=6. Queue: P1(rem=10), P3(6). P3 < P1. P3 runs.
t=8: P4 arrives (burst=5). P3_rem=4. 5 > 4 -> P3 keeps running.
t=12: P3 done. CT_P3=12. Queue: P1(rem=10), P4(5). P4 < P1. P4 runs.
t=17: P4 done. CT_P4=17. Only P1 left (rem=10). P1 runs.
t=27: P1 done. CT_P1=27.
steps.note: P2 has zero waiting time — it preempts immediately and runs to completion without interruption.

steps.title: Compute waiting times
steps.note: P1 has the highest waiting time (15ms) because it is preempted by three different processes before finally completing.

questions.formula_note: SRTF waiting time formula: WT = (Completion Time - Arrival Time) - Burst Time; preemption occurs whenever a newly arrived process has a shorter burst than the remaining time of the running process.
